verses.text: And, behold, there came a man of God out of Judah by the word of the LORD unto Bethel: and Jeroboam stood by the altar to burn incense. And he cried against the altar in the word of the LORD, and said, O altar, altar, thus saith the LORD; Behold, a child shall be born unto the house of David, Josiah by name; and upon thee shall he offer the priests of the high places that burn incense upon thee, and men's bones shall be burnt upon thee. And he gave a sign the same day, saying, This is the sign which the LORD hath spoken; Behold, the altar shall be rent, and the ashes that are upon it shall be poured out. Guidance: The most extraordinary prophecy in 1 Kings: a name spoken three centuries in advance. (1) The setting. Jeroboam is officiating personally at the Bethel altar — performing the priestly act of burning incense on the eighth-month festival he had invented (1 Kgs 12:33). The new northern cult is at its inaugural celebration. (2) The prophet. An anonymous 'man of God out of Judah.' He travels north to confront the new kingdom's apostasy. He is unnamed but armed with the LORD's word. (3) The address. 'O altar, altar' — he speaks not to the king but to the altar itself, treating the inanimate object as if it could hear. The address is theatrical and deliberate; the king and crowd are within earshot, but the prophet ignores the human audience and speaks directly to the central object of the false cult. (4) The prophecy. 'A child shall be born unto the house of David, Josiah by name; and upon thee shall he offer the priests of the high places that burn incense upon thee, and men's bones shall be burnt upon thee.' Three centuries later, around 622 BC, King Josiah of Judah will travel north to Bethel and do exactly this — desecrate the altar by burning human bones on it, slay the high-place priests, and break the altar to powder (2 Kgs 23:15-20). The name 'Josiah' is spoken in 931 BC and fulfilled in 622 BC. The chapter is preserving one of the Old Testament's most precise long-range predictive prophecies. (5) The sign. To validate the long-range prophecy, an immediate sign: the altar will be rent (split), and the ashes poured out — visible, falsifiable evidence the LORD has spoken. The chapter is teaching us a basic prophetic principle: prophecies can be both immediate-sign-verifiable and long-range. The same God who knows tomorrow knows three centuries from now; the immediate split-altar is the LORD's down payment on the eventual Josiah.

And it came to pass, when king Jeroboam heard the saying of the man of God, which had cried against the altar in Bethel, that he put forth his hand from the altar, saying, Lay hold on him. And his hand, which he put forth against him, dried up, so that he could not pull it in again to him. The altar also was rent, and the ashes poured out from the altar, according to the sign which the man of God had given by the word of the LORD. And the king answered and said unto the man of God, Intreat now the face of the LORD thy God, and pray for me, that my hand may be restored me again. And the man of God besought the LORD, and the king's hand was restored him again, and became as it was before. Guidance: Two visible signs and a moment of king-prophet exchange. (1) Jeroboam reaches out his hand — the gesture of arrest, ordering attendants to seize the prophet. His hand 'dried up' (vatiyivash) — paralyzed, withered, frozen mid-air. The arm he stretched against the LORD's messenger is the arm the LORD instantly stiffens. (2) The altar splits. The promised sign occurs as the prophet had spoken; ashes pour out into the dust. The Bethel altar — the centerpiece of Jeroboam's elaborate counterfeit cult — is publicly broken on its inaugural day. (3) The king's appeal. 'Intreat now the face of the LORD thy God, and pray for me.' The chapter is preserving a delicate detail. Jeroboam does not say 'my God' or 'our God'; he says 'thy God.' He acknowledges that the LORD belongs to the prophet, not to himself — the kingdom that has just rejected the LORD's Jerusalem temple cannot now address Him as theirs. The request is for personal relief, not corporate repentance. He wants his hand back; he is not surrendering his cult. (4) The intercession. The man of God prays; the king's hand is restored. The Hebrew vatashav — and it returned — to its previous state. The LORD's mercy operates even on a king who will not repent; the immediate relief is granted. But Jeroboam will not use this mercy as a doorway to the larger turning he should make. The chapter is showing us the difference between getting help from God and turning to God. Jeroboam wanted the first without doing the second. The chapter is teaching us a sobering truth about answered prayer: receiving a divine answer does not guarantee a transformed heart. The man whose hand the LORD has just healed will, in v33, return immediately to his idolatry.

And the king said unto the man of God, Come home with me, and refresh thyself, and I will give thee a reward. And the man of God said unto the king, If thou wilt give me half thine house, I will not go in with thee, neither will I eat bread nor drink water in this place: For so was it charged me by the word of the LORD, saying, Eat no bread, nor drink water, nor turn again by the same way that thou camest. So he went another way, and returned not by the way that he came to Bethel. Guidance: The refusal. Jeroboam's invitation is the ancient Near-East gesture of hospitality and reconciliation: come eat at my table, accept my gift, leave us on good terms. The hospitality offer is also a political pressure — the king wants to be seen as having patched up the public confrontation. The prophet's refusal is total and explicit. 'If thou wilt give me half thine house, I will not go in with thee, neither will I eat bread nor drink water in this place.' Three explicit prohibitions from the LORD: no eating bread, no drinking water, no returning by the same road. The reason for the prohibitions is not stated, but the symbolism is clear. To eat at Jeroboam's table or accept his hospitality would visibly compromise the public prophetic witness. The same prophet who has just cursed the altar would seem to be sharing fellowship with the king who built it. The refusal preserves the integrity of the prophecy. 'So he went another way, and returned not by the way that he came to Bethel.' He obeys completely. He leaves by a different route, refusing even the natural courtesy of retracing his steps. The chapter is teaching us a principle of prophetic obedience: when God forbids specific actions surrounding a public ministry-event, those prohibitions are integral to the witness, not peripheral. The prophet's refusal of the king's table is part of the message itself. The chapter is showing us a man of God who, at this point, has been completely faithful — prophesied the LORD's word, given the validating sign, prayed for the king's healing, refused the king's hospitality, returned by a different route. He is, at v10, the chapter's model of perfect obedience. The reader is at this point in no way prepared for what follows.

Now there dwelt an old prophet in Bethel; and his sons came and told him all the works that the man of God had done that day in Bethel: the words which he had spoken unto the king, them they told also to their father. And their father said unto them, What way went he? For his sons had seen what way the man of God went, which came from Judah. And he said unto his sons, Saddle me the ass. So they saddled him the ass: and he rode thereon, And went after the man of God, and found him sitting under an oak: and he said unto him, Art thou the man of God that camest from Judah? And he said, I am. Guidance: The old prophet of Bethel enters the narrative. His identity raises immediate questions. He is 'an old prophet' (navi echad zaqen) who lives in Bethel — the very town the man of God has just cursed. He has not himself confronted Jeroboam, despite living at the cult-center. His sons attend the day's events and report to him; his interest is keen. The chapter does not explicitly judge his earlier silence, but the contrast with the visiting prophet's bold confrontation is unmistakable. The old prophet is a man whose prophetic gift has perhaps gone dormant during years of accommodation to the northern cult — a possibility the rest of the chapter develops. He asks his sons which road the man of God took, saddles his donkey, and rides out to intercept him. The verification: 'Art thou the man of God that camest from Judah?' 'I am.' The two prophetic figures are now face to face. The chapter is teaching us, by structure, that the simplest test of any religious figure is the question the visiting prophet is willing to answer plainly: am I who I claim to be? The Judean prophet answers yes. The Bethel prophet's identity, we are about to learn, is more complicated than his title suggests. The chapter is showing us a meeting that will turn out badly. The reader is alerted by the careful narrative buildup that the old prophet's interest is not simple curiosity. Why is he chasing the visitor down? What does he want? The next paragraph will reveal.

Then he said unto him, Come home with me, and eat bread. And he said, I may not return with thee, nor go in with thee: neither will I eat bread nor drink water with thee in this place: For it was said to me by the word of the LORD, Thou shalt eat no bread nor drink water there, nor turn again to go by the way that thou camest. He said unto him, I am a prophet also as thou art; and an angel spake unto me by the word of the LORD, saying, Bring him back with thee into thine house, that he may eat bread and drink water. But he lied unto him. So he went back with him, and did eat bread in his house, and drank water. Guidance: The lie and the surrender. The old prophet repeats Jeroboam's invitation: come home, eat bread. The Judean prophet repeats his refusal — and this time gives his theological reason in detail: 'For it was said to me by the word of the LORD, Thou shalt eat no bread nor drink water there, nor turn again to go by the way that thou camest.' His refusal is grounded in direct divine prohibition. Then the lie: 'I am a prophet also as thou art; and an angel spake unto me by the word of the LORD, saying, Bring him back with thee into thine house, that he may eat bread and drink water.' The chapter's parenthetical comment is devastating: 'But he lied unto him.' The Hebrew kichesh lo — he deceived him. The old prophet manufactures a counter-revelation. He claims angelic authority. He frames his lie as a divine instruction that supersedes the original divine instruction. And the Judean prophet believes him. 'So he went back with him, and did eat bread in his house, and drank water.' The chapter is teaching us one of the hardest lessons in the Old Testament: a later prophetic claim never overrides an earlier direct divine instruction to the same person. The man of God should have answered: 'I have the LORD's word directly. If the LORD wishes to change that instruction, the LORD will tell me, not you. Until then, my original orders stand.' Instead he treats the old prophet's claim as sufficient grounds to act against what God told him personally. The principle is foundational for discernment. God does not contradict Himself; later messages from other people that 'release' you from earlier direct commands of God are nearly always deceptions. The chapter is also showing us the moral problem of the old prophet. Why did he lie? The text doesn't say explicitly. Some possibilities: jealousy (he wanted to associate himself with the visiting prophet's authority); loneliness (he wanted a meaningful conversation with a fellow prophet); residual conscience (perhaps he was testing whether the visiting prophet would maintain integrity). Whatever the motive, the result was catastrophic for the man of God.

Then he said unto him, Come home with me, and eat bread. And he said, I may not return with thee, nor go in with thee: neither will I eat bread nor drink water with thee in this place: For it was said to me by the word of the LORD, Thou shalt eat no bread nor drink water there, nor turn again to go by the way that thou camest. He said unto him, I am a prophet also as thou art; and an angel spake unto me by the word of the LORD, saying, Bring him back with thee into thine house, that he may eat bread and drink water. But he lied unto him. So he went back with him, and did eat bread in his house, and drank water. Guidance: The lie and the surrender. The old prophet repeats Jeroboam's invitation: come home, eat bread. The Judean prophet repeats his refusal — and this time gives his theological reason in detail: 'For it was said to me by the word of the LORD, Thou shalt eat no bread nor drink water there, nor turn again to go by the way that thou camest.' His refusal is grounded in direct divine prohibition. Then the lie: 'I am a prophet also as thou art; and an angel spake unto me by the word of the LORD, saying, Bring him back with thee into thine house, that he may eat bread and drink water.' The chapter's parenthetical comment is devastating: 'But he lied unto him.' The Hebrew kichesh lo — he deceived him. The old prophet manufactures a counter-revelation. He claims angelic authority. He frames his lie as a divine instruction that supersedes the original divine instruction. And the Judean prophet believes him. 'So he went back with him, and did eat bread in his house, and drank water.' The chapter is teaching us one of the hardest lessons in the Old Testament: a later prophetic claim never overrides an earlier direct divine instruction to the same person. The man of God should have answered: 'I have the LORD's word directly. If the LORD wishes to change that instruction, the LORD will tell me, not you. Until then, my original orders stand.' Instead he treats the old prophet's claim as sufficient grounds to act against what God told him personally. The principle is foundational for discernment. God does not contradict Himself; later messages from other people that 'release' you from earlier direct commands of God are nearly always deceptions. The chapter is also showing us the moral problem of the old prophet. Why did he lie? The text doesn't say explicitly. Some possibilities: jealousy (he wanted to associate himself with the visiting prophet's authority); loneliness (he wanted a meaningful conversation with a fellow prophet); residual conscience (perhaps he was testing whether the visiting prophet would maintain integrity). Whatever the motive, the result was catastrophic for the man of God.

And it came to pass, as they sat at the table, that the word of the LORD came unto the prophet that brought him back: And he cried unto the man of God that came from Judah, saying, Thus saith the LORD, Forasmuch as thou hast disobeyed the mouth of the LORD, and hast not kept the commandment which the LORD thy God commanded thee, But camest back, and hast eaten bread and drunk water in the place, of the which the LORD did say to thee, Eat no bread, and drink no water; thy carcase shall not come unto the sepulchre of thy fathers. Guidance: The word at the table — a remarkable moment. As the two prophets sit eating, the genuine word of the LORD arrives — through the lying prophet. The Hebrew vayehi devar-YHWH el ha-navi — and the word of the LORD came to the prophet (the deceiver). The LORD speaks through the man who has just lied, delivering the sentence on the man who has just disobeyed. The structural irony is striking. The old prophet, who fabricated a 'word of the LORD' to deceive his guest, now receives the actual word of the LORD that condemns his guest. He has, in effect, become the instrument of judgment on the man he tricked. The sentence: 'Thou hast disobeyed the mouth of the LORD, and hast not kept the commandment which the LORD thy God commanded thee… thy carcase shall not come unto the sepulchre of thy fathers.' The penalty: the man of God will not be buried with his family. In ancient near-east culture, being denied burial in the family tomb was a deep dishonor — the dead man would be remembered as an outsider, cut off from the ancestral line. The chapter is presenting the verdict with cold theological clarity. The man of God disobeyed direct divine instruction. The fact that he was deceived does not excuse him. He had the LORD's word personally; he chose to credit a counter-claim over his own original orders. The chapter is teaching us that prophetic deception does not absolve the deceived. We are responsible for our own discernment when we have direct divine instruction. The sentence is severe but proportional to the prophet's role; one who claims to speak for the LORD must take the LORD's commands with absolute seriousness, even when other claimants offer plausible-sounding overrides.

And it came to pass, after he had eaten bread, and after he had drunk, that he saddled for him the ass, to wit, for the prophet whom he had brought back. And when he was gone, a lion met him by the way, and slew him: and his carcase was cast in the way, and the ass stood by it, the lion also stood by the carcase. And, behold, men passed by, and saw the carcase cast in the way, and the lion standing by the carcase: and they came and told it in the city where the old prophet dwelt. Guidance: The lion's strange execution. The old prophet sends his guest on his way, providing him an ass. A short distance down the road, a lion meets the man of God and kills him. The carcase is left in the road; the donkey stands beside it; the lion also stands beside the carcase. The scene is uncanny. Lions, after killing prey, drag it off and consume it; they do not stand beside it. Donkeys, when a lion appears, flee in panic; they do not stand by the body of their rider. Yet here the donkey and the lion stand together, peacefully, as if a tableau has been arranged. The men passing by report the unusual sight to Bethel. The chapter is showing us divine providence operating in detail. The lion did not kill the man of God for its own hunger but at the LORD's appointing; once the appointed killing was done, the lion stopped functioning as a predator. The animal's normal behavior was suspended so that the witnesses could see clearly that this was no ordinary mauling. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD can suspend the ordinary instincts of His creatures when His purposes require an unmistakable sign. The story will be retold in Bethel as more than a tragic accident — it will be told as the LORD's judgment, visibly manifested. The donkey and the lion become living witnesses.

And when the prophet that brought him back from the way heard thereof, he said, It is the man of God, who was disobedient unto the word of the LORD: therefore the LORD hath delivered him unto the lion, which hath torn him, and slain him, according to the word of the LORD, which he spake unto him. And he spake to his sons, saying, Saddle me the ass. And they saddled him. And he went and found his carcase cast in the way, and the ass and the lion standing by the carcase: the lion had not eaten the carcase, nor torn the ass. And the prophet took up the carcase of the man of God, and laid it upon the ass, and brought it back: and the old prophet came to the city, to mourn and to bury him. And he laid his carcase in his own grave; and they mourned over him, saying, Alas, my brother! Guidance: The old prophet's retrieval and burial. (1) His immediate interpretation: 'It is the man of God, who was disobedient unto the word of the LORD: therefore the LORD hath delivered him unto the lion.' He recognizes the death as the precise fulfillment of the word he had just spoken at the table. The lie he told led to the disobedience; the disobedience led to the death; the death validated the second word. The old prophet has been an unwitting instrument of catastrophe. (2) The retrieval. He goes out, finds the tableau exactly as described — lion, donkey, body all standing peacefully — and the lion has neither eaten the man of God nor harmed the donkey. The supernatural restraint of the lion continues. The old prophet loads the body on his own donkey and brings it back to Bethel. (3) The burial. 'He laid his carcase in his own grave.' The Hebrew be-qivro — in his own grave, the grave he had prepared for himself. The old prophet places the man of God in the tomb intended for his own bones. 'They mourned over him, saying, Alas, my brother!' The mourning-cry hoi achi — alas, my brother. The old prophet treats the dead prophet as family in death. The chapter is showing us complex moral terrain. The old prophet is the cause of the man of God's death — and yet he is also the one who retrieves him with reverence, buries him in his own grave, and mourns him as a brother. Grief, guilt, recognition, and care are all present in his act. The chapter is honest about the entanglement; the prophet who deceives can also genuinely grieve when he sees the consequences of his deception.

The chapter is honest about the entanglement; the prophet who deceives can also genuinely grieve when he sees the consequences of his deception.

And when the prophet that brought him back from the way heard thereof, he said, It is the man of God, who was disobedient unto the word of the LORD: therefore the LORD hath delivered him unto the lion, which hath torn him, and slain him, according to the word of the LORD, which he spake unto him. And he spake to his sons, saying, Saddle me the ass. And they saddled him. And he went and found his carcase cast in the way, and the ass and the lion standing by the carcase: the lion had not eaten the carcase, nor torn the ass. And the prophet took up the carcase of the man of God, and laid it upon the ass, and brought it back: and the old prophet came to the city, to mourn and to bury him. And he laid his carcase in his own grave; and they mourned over him, saying, Alas, my brother! Guidance: The old prophet's retrieval and burial. (1) His immediate interpretation: 'It is the man of God, who was disobedient unto the word of the LORD: therefore the LORD hath delivered him unto the lion.' He recognizes the death as the precise fulfillment of the word he had just spoken at the table. The lie he told led to the disobedience; the disobedience led to the death; the death validated the second word. The old prophet has been an unwitting instrument of catastrophe. (2) The retrieval. He goes out, finds the tableau exactly as described — lion, donkey, body all standing peacefully — and the lion has neither eaten the man of God nor harmed the donkey. The supernatural restraint of the lion continues. The old prophet loads the body on his own donkey and brings it back to Bethel. (3) The burial. 'He laid his carcase in his own grave.' The Hebrew be-qivro — in his own grave, the grave he had prepared for himself. The old prophet places the man of God in the tomb intended for his own bones. 'They mourned over him, saying, Alas, my brother!' The mourning-cry hoi achi — alas, my brother. The old prophet treats the dead prophet as family in death. The chapter is showing us complex moral terrain. The old prophet is the cause of the man of God's death — and yet he is also the one who retrieves him with reverence, buries him in his own grave, and mourns him as a brother. Grief, guilt, recognition, and care are all present in his act. The chapter is honest about the entanglement; the prophet who deceives can also genuinely grieve when he sees the consequences of his deception.

And it came to pass, after he had buried him, that he spake to his sons, saying, When I am dead, then bury me in the sepulchre wherein the man of God is buried; lay my bones beside his bones: For the saying which he cried by the word of the LORD against the altar in Bethel, and against the houses of the high places which are in the cities of Samaria, shall surely come to pass. Guidance: The old prophet's posthumous request. He instructs his sons: bury me beside the man of God when I die. His reasoning is theological: 'the saying which he cried by the word of the LORD against the altar in Bethel, and against the houses of the high places which are in the cities of Samaria, shall surely come to pass.' The old prophet now believes the Judean prophet's altar-prophecy. When Josiah comes to desecrate northern tombs by burning human bones on the altars (per 2 Kgs 23:16-18), the man of God's grave will be visibly marked; the old prophet hopes his own bones, mingled with the man of God's, will share the same protection. The history bears out the calculation: 2 Kgs 23:18 records Josiah, having destroyed the altar and burned the bones of the surrounding tombs, sparing only the grave of the man of God and the old prophet of Bethel. 'Let no man move his bones. So they let his bones alone, with the bones of the prophet that came out of Samaria.' The old prophet's burial-request worked. The chapter is showing us partial repentance and partial faith. The old prophet now believes the word of the LORD his guest brought; he honors the dead messenger with shared burial; he plans for the long-range fulfillment. But he does not himself prophesy against the Bethel altar; he does not denounce Jeroboam's apostasy publicly; he remains a Bethel resident accommodating the northern cult. His response is to protect his own bones, not to denounce the system that produced the day's tragedy. The chapter is honest about this kind of compromised believer: knowing the LORD's word is true, regretting the failure that led to a brother prophet's death, yet not personally taking up the burden of public witness.

And it came to pass, after he had buried him, that he spake to his sons, saying, When I am dead, then bury me in the sepulchre wherein the man of God is buried; lay my bones beside his bones: For the saying which he cried by the word of the LORD against the altar in Bethel, and against the houses of the high places which are in the cities of Samaria, shall surely come to pass. Guidance: The old prophet's posthumous request. He instructs his sons: bury me beside the man of God when I die. His reasoning is theological: 'the saying which he cried by the word of the LORD against the altar in Bethel, and against the houses of the high places which are in the cities of Samaria, shall surely come to pass.' The old prophet now believes the Judean prophet's altar-prophecy. When Josiah comes to desecrate northern tombs by burning human bones on the altars (per 2 Kgs 23:16-18), the man of God's grave will be visibly marked; the old prophet hopes his own bones, mingled with the man of God's, will share the same protection. The history bears out the calculation: 2 Kgs 23:18 records Josiah, having destroyed the altar and burned the bones of the surrounding tombs, sparing only the grave of the man of God and the old prophet of Bethel. 'Let no man move his bones. So they let his bones alone, with the bones of the prophet that came out of Samaria.' The old prophet's burial-request worked. The chapter is showing us partial repentance and partial faith. The old prophet now believes the word of the LORD his guest brought; he honors the dead messenger with shared burial; he plans for the long-range fulfillment. But he does not himself prophesy against the Bethel altar; he does not denounce Jeroboam's apostasy publicly; he remains a Bethel resident accommodating the northern cult. His response is to protect his own bones, not to denounce the system that produced the day's tragedy. The chapter is honest about this kind of compromised believer: knowing the LORD's word is true, regretting the failure that led to a brother prophet's death, yet not personally taking up the burden of public witness.

After this thing Jeroboam returned not from his evil way, but made again of the lowest of the people priests of the high places: whosoever would, he consecrated him, and he became one of the priests of the high places. And this thing became sin unto the house of Jeroboam, even to cut it off, and to destroy it from off the face of the earth. Guidance: The chapter closes with Jeroboam's failure to repent. He has just seen the altar split, felt his own hand wither and be restored, heard the prophecy of Josiah's coming desecration, and (presumably) heard the news of the man of God's strange death on the road. None of it moves him. 'Jeroboam returned not from his evil way.' He continues installing non-Levitical priests at his high places. 'Whosoever would' (mi he-chaphetz — whoever wished) — anyone who wanted the priestly office could have it, regardless of Levitical descent. The priesthood was open for the asking. The chapter delivers the verdict: 'This thing became sin unto the house of Jeroboam, even to cut it off, and to destroy it from off the face of the earth.' The Hebrew le-hakhchid u-le-hashmid — to cut off and to destroy. Jeroboam's dynasty will not endure. His son Nadab will reign two years before being murdered by Baasha, who will exterminate every male of Jeroboam's house (1 Kgs 15:25-30). The chapter is teaching us the consequences of stubbornness. The LORD provided Jeroboam with multiple signs and warnings — the split altar, the withered hand, the visiting prophet's striking obedience, even the strange manner of the prophet's death. Each was a divine attempt to turn the king. None succeeded because Jeroboam's political calculation outweighed his spiritual response. He preferred the kingdom's preservation through false worship to its preservation through repentance. The same political calculation that built the calves would eventually destroy the dynasty that built them. The chapter is showing us, in compact form, the principle the rest of 1-2 Kings will trace at length: kings who manipulate religion for political ends destroy both the religion and themselves.
